week_headline: Ultra-processed foods and hidden metabolic blockers dominate health headlines as shoppers scrutinise labels and supplement shelves harder than ever

top10.claim: Best Maca Root Supplement ( 2026 ): Expert Reviewed
top10.verdict: Maca root has some real research behind it — small studies suggest it may support libido, mood during menopause, and energy — but the evidence is nowhere near strong enough to justify 'expert reviewed best supplement' marketing. Most trials are small, short, and industry-adjacent. It's probably safe in food amounts, but 'best supplement of 2026' is a commercial label, not a scientific one.
top10.verdict_highlight: Promising but evidence is still thin
sources: NIH National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health
top10.global_opinion: In Peru, maca is a traditional food crop eaten daily with no fanfare — locals would find the supplement hype quite baffling. In the EU, EFSA has not approved any health claims for maca, meaning European brands must be careful about what they promise on labels. In the US and UK, it sells freely as a supplement with minimal regulatory scrutiny, which is where a lot of the exaggerated marketing takes root.
top10.brit_take: It's a Peruvian root vegetable dressed up in a very expensive capsule. Your nan got the same energy from a strong cup of tea.

top10.claim: Surprising Foods That Are Actually Ultra Processed | The River 105 . 9
top10.verdict: The NOVA classification system — which defines ultra-processed foods — does catch some genuinely surprising items like flavoured yoghurts, mass-produced wholegrain bread, and plant-based meat alternatives. These aren't instinctively 'junk food' but do meet the technical criteria. That said, not all ultra-processed foods carry equal health risk, and lumping artisan granola with chicken nuggets under one label can oversimplify a nuanced picture.
top10.verdict_highlight: Ultra-processed is a spectrum, not one category
sources: NOVA Food Classification (Monteiro et al., BMJ 2019)
BMJ Open (2023 UPF and health outcomes review)
Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
top10.global_opinion: Brazil, where NOVA was developed, has embedded UPF awareness into national dietary guidelines — they actively encourage cooking from scratch as a health policy. In France, the Nutri-Score label helps consumers navigate processing levels, though it doesn't map perfectly onto NOVA. In the US, ultra-processed foods make up around 60% of caloric intake according to NIH data, and awareness of the NOVA framework is growing but still far behind public health messaging.
top10.brit_take: Turns out that 'healthy' supermarket granola you've been smugly spooning over yoghurt might be more processed than a biscuit. The label lied, but gently.

top10.claim: Not losing weight despite diet and exercise ? Longevity doctor shares 7 reasons you are not seeing results
top10.verdict: Several of the reasons longevity doctors cite for stalled weight loss — poor sleep, chronic stress, thyroid dysfunction, insulin resistance, and gut microbiome imbalance — are genuinely well-supported by research. The concern is when these are presented as a tidy checklist by a single expert, it can skip over the fact that weight regulation is deeply complex and individual. Good starting points, but treat it as a conversation opener, not a diagnosis.
top10.verdict_highlight: Real factors, but context always matters
top10.global_opinion: In Japan, where longevity research is deeply embedded in public health culture, lifestyle factors like stress and sleep are already integrated into routine medical conversations about weight. In Scandinavian countries, weight management guidance increasingly incorporates mental health and social determinants alongside diet and exercise. In the US and UK, the 'longevity doctor' format is popular but can blur the line between evidence-based medicine and wellness content marketing.
top10.brit_take: Seven reasons you're not losing weight sounds like a lot, but if number one turns out to be 'stress,' most of us already know the culprit and quietly hoped it wasn't.
